metadata.category: Arrays | Sorting | Sliding Window | Prefix Sum

content.problemStatement:
You are given a sequence A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ and an integer KKK.

Each subsequence of this sequence has a **beauty** associated with it which is equal to the sum of the absolute differences of every pair of elements in the subsequence. More formally, the **beauty** associated with an arbitrary subsequence Ai1,Ai2,…,AilA_{i_1}, A_{i_2}, \ldots, A_{i_l}Ai1​​,Ai2​​,…,Ail​​ is equal to the following summation:

∑a=1l∑b=al∣Aia−Aib∣\sum_{a=1}^{l} \sum_{b=a}^{l} |A_{i_a}-A_{i_b}|∑a=1l​∑b=al​∣Aia​​−Aib​​∣

where lll is the number of elements in the subsequence and i1,i2,…ili_1, i_2, \ldots i_li1​,i2​,…il​ are the indices of the elements in the original sequence AAA.

You need to find the minimum **beauty** across all the **beauties** that are associated to the subsequences of AAA which have exactly KKK elements.

A subsequence of a given sequence is a sequence that can be derived from the given sequence by deleting some or no elements without changing the order of the remaining elements.


Input Format


The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.




The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and KKK.




The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.





Output Format

For each test case, print a single line containing one integer ― the minimum **beauty** associated to a subsequence of AAA which has exactly KKK elements.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 4·10^5
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case:
- The first line contains two integers N and K.
- The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum possible beauty of any subsequence of length K.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1: The subsequence consisting of numbers 666, 444 and 555 has a beauty equal to 444 associated with it. This is the minimum possible beauty that is attainable across all subsequences with 333 elements.
Example case 2: The subsequence consisting of numbers 111, 111 and 111 has a beauty equal to 000 associated with it. This is the minimum possible beauty that is attainable across all subsequences with 333 elements.
Example case 3: The subsequence containing a single number 888 has a beauty equal to 000 associated with it. This is the minimum possible beauty that is attainable across all subsequences with only one element.
Example case 4: The subsequence consisting of numbers 12,5,43,7,312,5,43,7,312,5,43,7,3 has a beauty equal to 174174174 associated with it. This is the minimum possible beauty that is attainable across all subsequences with 555 elements.
Example case 5: The only subsequence we need to consider is the one that is equal to the whole sequence. That subsequence has a beauty of 121212 associated with it.

solution.approach:
The beauty of a subsequence depends only on the multiset of its values, not on their original positions. Therefore we may sort the whole array and choose any K elements. For a sorted list the sum of absolute differences of all unordered pairs in a window [l, r] (size K) can be expressed as:
    total = Σ_{i=l}^{r} A[i] * (2*i - 2*l - K + 1)
Using prefix sums P[i] = Σ_{0 ≤ j < i} A[j] and Q[i] = Σ_{0 ≤ j < i} A[j] * j we can compute for each window in O(1):
    sumS = P[r+1] - P[l]
    sumST = Q[r+1] - Q[l]
    total = 2 * sumST - (2*l + K - 1) * sumS
We slide the window over the sorted array, keep the minimum total, and output it. Sorting costs O(N log N); the sliding step is O(N). All arithmetic uses 128‑bit (or arbitrary‑precision) integers because the answer can exceed 64‑bit.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case (dominated by sorting).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the sorted array and two prefix‑sum arrays.
